description: Java / Spring security invariants. Conservative, low-false-positive patterns for the failure classes that recur in JVM code and are near-certain vulnerabilities on a single added line: the Log4Shell JNDI lookup string, command injection via Runtime.exec/ProcessBuilder string concatenation, unsafe native deserialization, Jackson polymorphic default typing (the CVE-2017-7525 family), permissive CORS (@CrossOrigin wildcard / bare annotation), Spring Security CSRF disabled, JPA/JDBC SQL built by string concatenation, JWT 'none'/unsigned acceptance, and XML factories created without XXE hardening. HARD only where a literal reading is almost always exploitable; everything that depends on out-of-line configuration (deserialization source, XML factory hardening, unsigned-JWT parse) is WARN. Rules: java-log4shell-jndi-lookup, java-runtime-exec-string-concat, java-runtime-exec-bare, java-native-deserialization, java-jackson-enable-default-typing, java-jackson-activate-default-typing, java-cors-wildcard-origin, java-cors-annotation-bare, java-spring-csrf-disabled, java-sql-string-concat, java-jwt-none-algorithm, java-jwt-unsigned-parse, java-xml-factory-xxe.

rules.//: Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228): the literal `${jndi:ldap://...}` (or rmi/dns/etc.) lookup string in source — either a forgotten exploit-test payload or an attacker-controlled value being assembled. A legitimate property placeholder like `${user.home}` does NOT start with `jndi:`, so the protocol list keeps this near-zero FP. Fix: never log/interpolate untrusted input into a format string, and run a patched logging library (log4j >= 2.17 / disable message lookups).
rules.message: Log4Shell JNDI lookup string (${jndi:ldap://...}) in source — this is an exploit payload or an attacker-influenced lookup. Remove it, never interpolate untrusted input into a log/format string, and run a patched logging library with message lookups disabled.

rules.//: Command injection: a string literal concatenated with a variable passed straight to Runtime.exec("cmd " + arg) or ProcessBuilder. The shell/exec receives an attacker-influenced command line. The pattern requires `"..." +` immediately after `.exec(`, so an argv array `exec(new String[]{...})` or a constant `exec("ls -la")` does NOT match. Fix: pass an argv array (no shell), validate/allowlist the argument, and avoid `sh -c`.
rules.message: Runtime.exec("..." + var) builds a command line by concatenation — command injection. Pass an argv array (String[]), never a concatenated string; validate/allowlist the argument and avoid `sh -c`.

rules.//: Companion WARN to java-runtime-exec-string-concat: any Runtime.getRuntime().exec(...) call, even with a single string variable, hands the command to the OS and is worth a second look — the variable may itself be concatenated upstream. WARN (not HARD) because exec with a fully-static, trusted command is legitimate. Confirm the argument is not attacker-influenced; prefer ProcessBuilder with an argv array.
rules.message: Runtime.getRuntime().exec(...) — confirm the command and arguments are not attacker-influenced. Prefer ProcessBuilder with an argv array over a shell command string.

rules.//: Java native deserialization: `new ObjectInputStream(...)` reads a serialized object graph and will instantiate any class on the classpath, the root of the gadget-chain RCE family. WARN because deserializing fully-trusted, in-process data is legitimate — but readObject() on anything crossing a trust boundary (HTTP body, cache, queue, file upload) is a critical sink. Fix: avoid Java serialization for untrusted data (use JSON with explicit types), or install a strict ObjectInputFilter allowlist.
rules.message: new ObjectInputStream(...) deserializes a Java object graph — if the source is untrusted this is a gadget-chain RCE sink. Avoid native serialization for untrusted data (prefer JSON with explicit types) or set a strict ObjectInputFilter allowlist.

rules.//: Jackson polymorphic typing (CVE-2017-7525 family): `enableDefaultTyping()` makes the mapper embed and trust a `@class` in the JSON, letting an attacker instantiate arbitrary gadget classes during deserialization — RCE. The legacy enableDefaultTyping() is deprecated precisely because it is unsafe with no validation. HARD: there is no safe way to call the un-validated form on untrusted input. Fix: register specific subtypes with @JsonSubTypes, or use activateDefaultTyping with a tightly-scoped PolymorphicTypeValidator.
rules.message: ObjectMapper.enableDefaultTyping() enables unvalidated polymorphic deserialization — an RCE vector (CVE-2017-7525 family). Register explicit subtypes (@JsonSubTypes) or use activateDefaultTyping with a strict PolymorphicTypeValidator.

rules.//: WARN companion: `activateDefaultTyping(...)` is the modern replacement that REQUIRES a PolymorphicTypeValidator, so it CAN be used safely — but only if the validator is tightly scoped. A LaissezFaireSubTypeValidator (or an over-broad allowOf) is as dangerous as the deprecated form. WARN so the validator scope is reviewed at the call site rather than blocked outright.
rules.message: ObjectMapper.activateDefaultTyping(...) enables polymorphic deserialization — safe ONLY with a tightly-scoped PolymorphicTypeValidator. Confirm the validator allowlists specific base types; a LaissezFaireSubTypeValidator is an RCE vector.

rules.//: Permissive CORS: `@CrossOrigin(origins = "*")` (or `@CrossOrigin("*")`, or a `{"*"}` array of one) reflects any origin, which combined with credentials defeats the same-origin policy. HARD because a literal `"*"` origin is almost never intended on a real endpoint. The optional `origins=` and `[{\s]*` cover the common single-value forms; a wildcard hidden inside a multi-element list is intentionally NOT matched (kept conservative). Fix: enumerate exact allowed origins.
rules.message: @CrossOrigin with a wildcard origin ("*") reflects any site and breaks the same-origin policy (fatal with allowCredentials). Enumerate exact allowed origins instead of "*".

rules.//: Bare `@CrossOrigin` with no arguments defaults to allowing ALL origins (and all headers), the same allow-everything posture as `"*"` but easy to miss because it looks innocuous. The negative lookahead `(?!\s*\()` fires only when the annotation has no argument list, so `@CrossOrigin(origins = "https://app.example.com")` and the wildcard form (handled by java-cors-wildcard-origin) are NOT double-flagged. WARN so a deliberately-open public endpoint can be confirmed.
rules.message: Bare @CrossOrigin (no arguments) allows all origins and headers by default. Specify the exact allowed origins, e.g. @CrossOrigin(origins = "https://app.example.com").

rules.//: Spring Security CSRF disabled: `.csrf().disable()`, the lambda `.csrf(c -> c.disable())`, and the Spring Security 6 method-reference `.csrf(AbstractHttpConfigurer::disable)` all turn off CSRF protection for the filter chain. WARN because a stateless, token-only (no cookie) API legitimately disables CSRF — but disabling it on a cookie-session app reopens CSRF. The three alternations match the disable forms while `.csrf(Customizer.withDefaults())` (CSRF kept ON) does NOT match. Confirm the app is truly stateless before disabling.
rules.message: Spring Security CSRF protection disabled (.csrf().disable() / csrf(c -> c.disable()) / csrf(AbstractHttpConfigurer::disable)). Only acceptable for a stateless token-only API with no cookie session; otherwise this reopens CSRF — keep it enabled.

rules.//: SQL built by string concatenation passed to a JPA/JDBC call: createQuery/createNativeQuery/prepareStatement/executeQuery/executeUpdate with `"..." + var`. The literal-then-`+` shape is the injection tell. A parameterized call — `prepareStatement("... WHERE id = ?")` or `createQuery("... WHERE id = :id")` — has no `+` after the string and does NOT match. Fix: use bind parameters (`?` / named `:param` / setParameter), never string-built SQL/JPQL.
rules.message: SQL/JPQL built by concatenation ("..." + var) passed to createQuery/createNativeQuery/prepareStatement/executeQuery/executeUpdate — SQL injection. Use bind parameters (? / :name / setParameter), never string-built queries.

rules.//: JWT 'none' / unsigned: `SignatureAlgorithm.NONE` declares the token unsigned, so a forged token validates — an auth bypass. HARD because there is no legitimate reason to sign or accept with the none algorithm. Fix: sign with HS256/RS256/ES256/EdDSA and verify against a fixed expected algorithm.
rules.message: JWT SignatureAlgorithm.NONE produces/accepts an unsigned token — any forged token validates (auth bypass). Sign and verify with a fixed strong algorithm (HS256/RS256/ES256/EdDSA).

rules.//: `parseClaimsJwt(...)` (jjwt) parses an UNSIGNED JWT — note the trailing `Jwt`, vs the signed `parseClaimsJws`. Accepting an unsigned token means the signature is never checked. WARN (not HARD) because a few flows legitimately read unsigned claims, but it is almost always a mistake for an auth token. Use parseClaimsJws (or parseSignedClaims) with the verification key.
rules.message: parseClaimsJwt(...) parses an UNSIGNED JWT — the signature is never verified. For auth tokens use parseClaimsJws(...) / parseSignedClaims(...) with the verification key.

rules.//: XXE: an XML parser factory created via newInstance() defaults to resolving external entities, so untrusted XML can read local files or trigger SSRF/DoS (billion laughs). The factory line itself is where hardening must be applied, but the secure setFeature(...) calls live on later lines a single-line regex cannot confirm — so this is WARN, a prompt to verify the hardening exists, not a block. Fix: setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true) (or XMLConstants.FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING) and disable external general/parameter entities.
rules.message: XML parser factory (DocumentBuilderFactory/SAXParserFactory/XMLInputFactory) created — confirm XXE hardening on the factory: disallow-doctype-decl / FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING and disable external general+parameter entities before parsing untrusted XML.
